lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Welcome, dear Rosencrantz and Guildenstern!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Moreover that we much did long to see you,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The need we have to use you did provoke

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Our hasty sending. Something have you heard

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Of Hamlet’s transformation; so call it,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Sith nor the exterior nor the inward man

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Resembles that it was. What it should be,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: More than his father’s death, that thus hath put him

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: So much from the understanding of himself,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: I cannot dream of: I entreat you both,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: That, being of so young days brought up with him,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And sith so neighbour’d to his youth and havior,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: That you vouchsafe your rest here in our court

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Some little time: so by your companies

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To draw him on to pleasures, and to gather,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: So much as from occasion you may glean,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Whether aught, to us unknown, afflicts him thus,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: That, open’d, lies within our remedy.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Good gentlemen, he hath much talk’d of you;

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: And sure I am two men there are not living

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: To whom he more adheres. If it will please you

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: To show us so much gentry and good will

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: As to expend your time with us awhile,

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: For the supply and profit of our hope,

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Your visitation shall receive such thanks

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: As fits a king’s remembrance.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Both your majesties

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Might, by the sovereign power you have of us,

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Put your dread pleasures more into command

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Than to entreaty.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: But we both obey,

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: And here give up ourselves, in the full bent

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: To lay our service freely at your feet,

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: To be commanded.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Thanks, Rosencrantz and gentle Guildenstern.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Thanks, Guildenstern and gentle Rosencrantz:

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: And I beseech you instantly to visit

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: My too much changed son. Go, some of you,

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: And bring these gentlemen where Hamlet is.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: Heavens make our presence and our practices

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: Pleasant and helpful to him!

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Ay, amen!

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: The ambassadors from Norway, my good lord,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Are joyfully return’d.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Thou still hast been the father of good news.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Have I, my lord? I assure my good liege,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: I hold my duty, as I hold my soul,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Both to my God and to my gracious king:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And I do think, or else this brain of mine

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Hunts not the trail of policy so sure

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: As it hath used to do, that I have found

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: The very cause of Hamlet’s lunacy.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: O, speak of that; that do I long to hear.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Give first admittance to the ambassadors;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: My news shall be the fruit to that great feast.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Thyself do grace to them, and bring them in.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: He tells me, my dear Gertrude, he hath found

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The head and source of all your son’s distemper.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: I doubt it is no other but the main;

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: His father’s death, and our o’erhasty marriage.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Well, we shall sift him.

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: Most fair return of greetings and desires.

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: Upon our first, he sent out to suppress

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: His nephew’s levies; which to him appear’d

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: To be a preparation ’gainst the Polack;

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: But, better look’d into, he truly found

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: It was against your highness: whereat grieved,

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: That so his sickness, age and impotence

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: Was falsely borne in hand, sends out arrests

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: On Fortinbras; which he, in brief, obeys;

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: Receives rebuke from Norway, and in fine

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: Makes vow before his uncle never more

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: To give the assay of arms against your majesty.

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: Whereon old Norway, overcome with joy,

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: Gives him three thousand crowns in annual fee,

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: And his commission to employ those soldiers,

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: So levied as before, against the Polack:

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: With an entreaty, herein further shown,

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: That it might please you to give quiet pass

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: Through your dominions for this enterprise,

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: On such regards of safety and allowance

lines.speaker: VOLTIMAND
lines.text: As therein are set down.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: It likes us well;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And at our more consider’d time well read,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Answer, and think upon this business.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Meantime we thank you for your well-took labour:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Go to your rest; at night we’ll feast together:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Most welcome home!

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: This business is well ended.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: My liege, and madam, to expostulate

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: What majesty should be, what duty is,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Why day is day, night night, and time is time,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Were nothing but to waste night, day and time.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Therefore, since brevity is the soul of wit,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And tediousness the limbs and outward flourishes,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: I will be brief: your noble son is mad:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Mad call I it; for, to define true madness,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: What is’t but to be nothing else but mad?

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: But let that go.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: More matter, with less art.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Madam, I swear I use no art at all. That he is

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: mad, ’tis true: ’tis true ’tis pity; And pity ’tis ’tis true:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: a foolish figure; But farewell it, for I will use no art.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Mad let us grant him, then: and now remains That we find

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: out the cause of this effect, Or rather say, the cause of

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: this defect, For this effect defective comes by cause: Thus it remains,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: and the remainder thus. Perpend. I have a daughter—have while she is

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: mine— Who, in her duty and obedience, mark, Hath given me this:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: now gather, and surmise. “To the celestial and my soul’s idol, the

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: most beautified Ophelia,”— That’s an ill phrase, a vile phrase; “beautified” is

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: a vile phrase: but you shall hear. Thus: “In her excellent white

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: bosom, these, etc.”

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Came this from Hamlet to her?

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Good madam, stay awhile; I will be faithful. “Doubt thou the stars

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: are fire; Doubt that the sun doth move; Doubt truth to be

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: a liar; But never doubt I love. “O dear Ophelia, I am

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: ill at these numbers; I have not art to reckon my groans:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: but that I love thee best, O most best, believe it. Adieu.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: “Thine evermore most dear lady, whilst this machine is to him, Hamlet.”

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: This, in obedience, hath my daughter shown me, And more above, hath

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: his solicitings, As they fell out by time, by means and place,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: All given to mine ear.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: But how hath she

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Received his love?

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: What do you think of me?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: As of a man faithful and honourable.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: I would fain prove so. But what might you think,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: When I had seen this hot love on the wing—

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: As I perceived it, I must tell you that,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Before my daughter told me—what might you,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Or my dear majesty your queen here, think,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: If I had play’d the desk or table-book,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Or given my heart a winking, mute and dumb,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Or look’d upon this love with idle sight;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: What might you think? No, I went round to work,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And my young mistress thus I did bespeak:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: “Lord Hamlet is a prince, out of thy star;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: This must not be:” and then I precepts gave her,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: That she should lock herself from his resort,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Admit no messengers, receive no tokens.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Which done, she took the fruits of my advice;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And he, repulsed—a short tale to make—

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Fell into a sadness, then into a fast,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Thence to a watch, thence into a weakness,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Thence to a lightness, and, by this declension,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Into the madness wherein now he raves,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And all we mourn for.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Do you think ’tis this?

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: It may be, very likely.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Hath there been such a time—I’d fain know that—

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: That I have positively said “ ’Tis so,”

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: When it proved otherwise?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Not that I know.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Take this from this, if this be otherwise:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: If circumstances lead me, I will find

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Where truth is hid, though it were hid indeed

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Within the centre.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: How may we try it further?

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: You know, sometimes he walks four hours together

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Here in the lobby.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: So he does indeed.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: At such a time I’ll loose my daughter to him:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Be you and I behind an arras then;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Mark the encounter: if he love her not

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And be not from his reason fall’n thereon,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Let me be no assistant for a state,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: But keep a farm and carters.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: We will try it.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: But, look, where sadly the poor wretch comes reading.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Away, I do beseech you, both away:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: I’ll board him presently.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Well, God-a-mercy.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Do you know me, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Excellent well; you are a fishmonger.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Not I, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Then I would you were so honest a man.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Honest, my lord!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Ay, sir; to be honest, as this world goes, is to be

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: one man picked out of ten thousand.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: That’s very true, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: For if the sun breed maggots in a dead dog, being a

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: god kissing carrion—Have you a daughter?

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: I have, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Let her not walk i’ the sun: conception is a blessing: but

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: not as your daughter may conceive. Friend, look to’t.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: How say you by that? Still harping on my daughter: yet he

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: knew me not at first; he said I was a fishmonger: he

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: is far gone, far gone: and truly in my youth I suffered

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: much extremity for love; very near this. I’ll speak to him again.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: What do you read, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Words, words, words.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: What is the matter, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Between who?

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: I mean, the matter that you read, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Slanders, sir: for the satirical rogue says here that old men have

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: grey beards, that their faces are wrinkled, their eyes purging thick amber

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: and plum-tree gum and that they have a plentiful lack of wit,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: together with most weak hams: all which, sir, though I most powerfully

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: and potently believe, yet I hold it not honesty to have it

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: thus set down, for yourself, sir, should be old as I am,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: if like a crab you could go backward.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Though this be madness, yet there is method in’t. Will you walk

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: out of the air, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Into my grave.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Indeed, that is out o’ the air. How pregnant sometimes his replies

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: are! a happiness that often madness hits on, which reason and sanity

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: could not so prosperously be delivered of. I will leave him, and

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: suddenly contrive the means of meeting between him and my daughter.—My honourable

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: lord, I will most humbly take my leave of you.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: You cannot, sir, take from me any thing that I will more

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: willingly part withal: except my life, except my life, except my life.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Fare you well, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: These tedious old fools!

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: You go to seek the Lord Hamlet; there he is.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: To Polonius. God save you, sir!

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: My honoured lord!

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: My most dear lord!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: My excellent good friends! How dost thou, Guildenstern? Ah, Rosencrantz! Good lads,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: how do ye both?

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: As the indifferent children of the earth.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: Happy, in that we are not over-happy;

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: On fortune’s cap we are not the very button.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Nor the soles of her shoe?

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Neither, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Then you live about her waist, or in the middle of her favours?

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: ’Faith, her privates we.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: In the secret parts of fortune? O, most true; she is a

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: strumpet. What’s the news?

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: None, my lord, but that the world’s grown honest.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Then is doomsday near: but your news is not true. Let me

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: question more in particular: what have you, my good friends, deserved at

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: the hands of fortune, that she sends you to prison hither?

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: Prison, my lord!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Denmark’s a prison.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Then is the world one.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: A goodly one; in which there are many confines, wards and dungeons,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Denmark being one o’ the worst.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: We think not so, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why, then, ’tis none to you; for there is nothing either good

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: or bad, but thinking makes it so: to me it is a

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: prison.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Why then, your ambition makes it one; ’tis too narrow for your mind.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O God, I could be bounded in a nut-shell and count myself

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: a king of infinite space, were it not that I have bad

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: dreams.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: Which dreams indeed are ambition, for the very substance of the ambitious

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: is merely the shadow of a dream.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: A dream itself is but a shadow.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Truly, and I hold ambition of so airy and light a quality

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: that it is but a shadow’s shadow.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Then are our beggars bodies, and our monarchs and outstretched heroes the

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: beggars’ shadows. Shall we to the court? for, by my fay, I

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: cannot reason.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: We’ll wait upon you.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: No such matter: I will not sort you with the rest of

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: my servants, for, to speak to you like an honest man, I

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: am most dreadfully attended. But, in the beaten way of friendship, what

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: make you at Elsinore?

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: To visit you, my lord; no other occasion.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Beggar that I am, I am even poor in thanks; but I

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: thank you: and sure, dear friends, my thanks are too dear a

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: halfpenny. Were you not sent for? Is it your own inclining? Is

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: it a free visitation? Come, deal justly with me: come, come; nay,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: speak.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: What should we say, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why, any thing, but to the purpose. You were sent for; and

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: there is a kind of confession in your looks which your modesties

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: have not craft enough to colour: I know the good king and

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: queen have sent for you.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: To what end, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That you must teach me. But let me conjure you, by the

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: rights of our fellowship, by the consonancy of our youth, by the

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: obligation of our ever-preserved love, and by what more dear a better

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: proposer could charge you withal, be even and direct with me, whether

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: you were sent for, or no?

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: What say you?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Nay, then, I have an eye of you.—If you love me, hold not off.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: My lord, we were sent for.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I will tell you why; so shall my anticipation prevent your discovery,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: and your secrecy to the king and queen moult no feather. I

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: have of late—but wherefore I know not—lost all my mirth, forgone all

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: custom of exercises; and indeed it goes so heavily with my disposition

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: that this goodly frame, the earth, seems to me a sterile promontory,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: this most excellent canopy, the air, look you, this brave o’erhanging firmament,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: this majestical roof fretted with golden fire, why, it appears no other

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: thing to me than a foul and pestilent congregation of vapours. What

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: a piece of work is a man! how noble in reason! how

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: infinite in faculty! in form and moving how express and admirable! in

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: action how like an angel! in apprehension how like a god! the

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: beauty of the world! the paragon of animals! And yet, to me,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: what is this quintessence of dust? man delights not me: no, nor

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: woman neither, though by your smiling you seem to say so.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: My lord, there was no such stuff in my thoughts.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why did you laugh then, when I said “man delights not me”?

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: To think, my lord, if you delight not in man, what lenten

stage_directions.text: Enter King, Queen, Rosencrantz, Guildenstern, and Attendants.
Exeunt Rosencrantz, Guildenstern, and some Attendants.
Reenter Polonius, with Voltimand and Cornelius.
Welcome, my good friends! Say, Voltimand, what from our brother Norway?
Pointing to his head and shoulder.
O, give me leave: How does my good Lord Hamlet?

You are welcome, masters; welcome, all. I am glad to see thee well. Welcome, good friends. O, my old friend! thy face is valanced since I saw thee last: comest thou to beard me in Denmark? What, my young lady and mistress! By’r lady, your ladyship is nearer to heaven than when I saw you last, by the altitude of a chopine. Pray God, your voice, like apiece of uncurrent gold, be not cracked within the ring. Masters, you are all welcome. We’ll e’en to’t like French falconers, fly at any thing we see: we’ll have a speech straight: come, give us a taste of your quality; come, a passionate speech.
Exit Polonius with all the Players but the First.

glosses.phrase: of so young days brought up with him
three thousand crowns in annual fee
brevity is the soul of wit
glosses.definition: conciseness is the essence of intelligence

unfledged hawks (the child actors fashionable in 1600s London)
biblical judge who sacrificed his daughter

annotations.title: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern: the instruments of friendship
annotations.body: Claudius and Gertrude's charge to Hamlet's school-friends is a small masterpiece of political language. They are summoned 'of so young days brought up with him' — the friendship is real, which is why its weaponization will be ethically grave — but the request is surveillance with the manners of hospitality. Hamlet will later see through it instantly: 'Were you not sent for?' The scene also shows the play's mature political intelligence: Claudius does not command, he asks, and he frames the asking as a gift the young men may decline. The very shape of courtly tyranny, Stoppard's Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead would later argue, is that no one is ever quite forced.
sources: Stephen Greenblatt, Will in the World (2004)

annotations.title: Polonius's diagnosis and the politics of madness
annotations.body: Polonius's long-winded display before the King — 'brevity is the soul of wit' delivered in the least brief speech imaginable — is a set-piece of dramatic irony. Shakespeare plays it for comedy while making the political stakes serious: the chamberlain is committing royal resources to the theory that Hamlet is lovesick, and the decision to 'loose' Ophelia as bait follows directly. Polonius's reading of the private letter to Ophelia — 'Doubt thou the stars are fire' — violates every courtesy and weaponizes a love poem for intelligence. It is Shakespeare's finest portrait of the officious counselor whose ambition to serve has eaten his conscience.

annotations.title: 'What a piece of work is a man'
annotations.body: Hamlet's paean to human nature — 'in action how like an angel! in apprehension how like a god!' — would be Renaissance commonplace if he left it there. The speech's power lies in its collapse: 'And yet, to me, what is this quintessence of dust?' In a handful of lines Hamlet traverses the distance from Pico della Mirandola's Oration on the Dignity of Man to Ecclesiastes's 'all is vanity.' Modern productions play the passage ironically; original Elizabethan audiences would have heard it as a genuine hymn to humanism undercut by the speaker's own grief. The speech announces a philosophical problem the play will not solve: why is a creature capable of so much unable to act even once?

annotations.title: The second soliloquy: the player and the prince
annotations.body: Hamlet's soliloquy after the player's weeping speech about Hecuba is the play's most self-lacerating. The comparison is cruel: a professional actor, prompted by nothing, can summon tears for a fiction; Hamlet, prompted by a father's murder, has summoned nothing at all. The accusation — 'rogue and peasant slave,' 'muddy-mettled rascal,' 'John-a-dreams' — is both genuine self-contempt and a kind of self-flagellating performance, an attempt to will himself into feeling by describing his insufficiency. The soliloquy's turn at 'About, my brain!' is the play's first concrete plan: the Mousetrap. Hamlet discovers, under the pressure of shame, that he cannot act immediately but he can direct a play.
sources: Stephen Orgel, The Illusion of Power (1975)
Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

trials.prompt: Why do the King and Queen summon Rosencrantz and Guildenstern?
options: To serve as witnesses at Hamlet's trial.
To befriend Hamlet and discover the cause of his madness.
To deliver a diplomatic letter to Wittenberg.
To serve as guards during the ambassadors' visit.

trials.prompt: When Polonius says 'brevity is the soul of wit,' what is the dramatic irony?
options: He is speaking Latin, though the audience hears English.
He delivers the maxim in the most long-winded speech in the play.
He is quoting Hamlet's private letter without permission.
He is teaching Ophelia how to speak concisely to the King.

trials.prompt: How does Hamlet's speech 'What a piece of work is a man' conclude?
options: In an ecstatic affirmation of human nobility.
By collapsing into the question 'what is this quintessence of dust?' — undercutting the praise.
With a prayer that God restore Denmark's peace.
With a vow to kill Claudius before nightfall.

trials.prompt: What troubles Hamlet about the player's speech on Hecuba?
options: That the actor has misquoted the Aeneid.
That a hired player can summon real tears for a fiction, while he has summoned none for his real father's murder.
That the speech contains unflattering references to Claudius.
That the player refuses to join Hamlet's plot against the King.

trials.prompt: What concrete plan does Hamlet form by the end of the scene?
options: To stab Claudius at the next banquet.
To stage a play that re-enacts his father's murder and observe Claudius's reaction.
To flee to England with Horatio.
To confront Gertrude in her bedchamber.

trials.prompt: Why does Hamlet admit he must first test the ghost's testimony through the play?
options: Because Horatio demanded evidence before joining the plot.
Because the ghost 'may be the devil' who 'hath power to assume a pleasing shape' to damn him.
Because Claudius has sworn an oath of innocence in public.
Because the Queen has forbidden him to act without proof.
